This is definitely a good thing to have. Personally, I think sanitize_tx_data and just having it compare the outputs, if a different address type is on both, raise a ValueError. I think that will break fewer tests, can still be tested easily, and isn't overly invasive. I don't see why a user would ever want to attempt mixing mainnet and testnet addresses.

I would keep the assumption of which version to the addresses themselves. Not sure off the top of my head if outputs are typed and the type can return the chain. That could be a very explicit way for a user to implement it, if he or she wanted. `outputs = ('mainnet_address', 10000)`, `assert outputs.target_chain == 'mainnet'.`, etc. Also forgot if outputs are bundled or each have individual entries, so possibly that area could be another spot to implement it in.

Thanks for spotting this.

I looked up what os.getppid is, and the Python 3 documentation says:

>Changed in version 3.2: Added support for Windows.

And the Python 2 documentation says:

>Availability: Unix.

So I tried the code above and it fixes the crash.

You are right that there seems to be `Process.ppid()` in [psutil](https://pythonhosted.org/psutil/#psutil.Process.ppid), so it may be possible to rewrite it with that, but I feel like it may get messy quickly (nested Process() calls).

Anyway, I'll be glad either way if it gets fixed in the upstream so that I can have a reproducible environment without hacks :)

I'm very close to pushing a first branch for review, as all the features that don't involve indexed parameters have now been implemented.

However, the issue now becomes how to deal with index ranges. Because in order to fully benefit from `sympy`'s symbolic capabilities, indices need to know their range. This range can be infered from the data, but, as stated earlier, this has to be done explicitly, not implicit. For example, in
```python
x, y = variables('x, y', indexed=True)
i, = indices('i')

model = {y[i]: a * x[i] + b}
fit = Fit(model, x=xdata, y=ydata)
```
we could have `Fit` look at the data, and set the range of `i` based on that. However, that would mean that `i.range` now has a value, whereas I as the user never set it at all. And as the zen of python dictates: explicit is better than implicit.

In the example above, one can already do the following: `i, = indices('i', range=len(xdata))`. This is not a bad solution, since it forces the user to think about his shapes, and whether they are sensible.

A convenience method I like, might be to do make this work:
```python
x, (i, j), y, (i, j) = variables('x[i, j], y[i, j]', x=xdata.shape, y=ydata.shape)
```
This would do all the range setting in one line, and would therefore allow to check if the two shapes do indeed match.

It shouldn't matter since the function is recursive. The only problem I found with proposed approach is that if you serialize `{1: []}` and then deserialize you get `{1: {}}` since detecting whenever the node is a list is done by checking if the first subnode `key == '0'`.

That is exactly what I thought of. Keeping the steps **frequent itemset generation** and **rule generation** separated makes perfect sense in terms of flexibility and further scalability. 

To not get confused with the terminology and allow further integration of other algorithms, I would suggest the following structure:

## Types of classes used (a.k.a. interfaces)

> Frequent Itemset Genarator

Classes that take a one-hot-encoded pandas DataFrame and generate a pandas DataFrame of frequent itemsets - therefore implement the `generate_frequent_itemsets(df, minsup, ...)` method. 

> Association Rule Generator


Classes that take a pandas DataFrame of frequent itemsets and generate a pandas DataFrame of association rules - thefore implement the `generate_association_rules(df, minconf, ...)`<sup>[1] </sup> method.


## Module Organisation
    ├── ...                       <- other mlxtend modules
    └── frequent_patterns         <- rename "association" to "frequent_patterns" (optionally)
        ├── apriori.py            <- frequent itemset generator implemented
        ├── association_rules.py  <- association rule generator implemented
        └── fpgrowth.py           <- frequent itemset generator implemented

## Open Questions

- [ ] Attributes of the frequent itemsets DataFrame (is "length" of current [apriori.py](https://github.com/rasbt/mlxtend/blob/master/mlxtend/association/apriori.py) needed?)
- [ ] Attributes and column types of the association rules DataFrame <sup>[2] suggested structure</sup>



These would be my two cents for today :)
___
[1] method parameter potentially later change to `generate_association_rules(..., criterion={'minconf', 'lift', ...}, threshold, ...) `

[2]

| ID | condition | consequent | support | confidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|0|[Bread, Milk]|[Eggs]|0.5|0.9|
|1|[Diapers]|[Beer]|0.6|0.8|
|...|...|...|...|...|

Okay. I've figured it out. The issue is triggered by the output capturing. When pytest runs, it replaces `sys.stdin` with a wrapper in order to capture standard input, which affects the logic in `ldap3.utils.config`. If I run `pytest` with `-s` (don't capture input/output), the tests pass once again.

The [offending logic is here](https://github.com/cannatag/ldap3/blob/12106a9467162767c5772544957bd822eef37a02/ldap3/utils/config.py#L94-L99). When pytest is capturing the stdin, the first branch is False, so the default encoding gets set to `sys.getdefaultencoding()`, which is ASCII.

So even though in the usage, I'm passing values as unicode, then in `parse_filter`, they're getting encoded as UTF-8 and then later attempted to be decoded using `sys.getdefaultencoding()`, and all as part of the client handling (not encoding for the server yet).

As I usually travel between locations that are properly recognised automatically, I have configured to run automatically, as follows:

I have created a `/home/myuser/.local/bin/tzupdate.service` file, with the content

```
[Unit]
Description=Time zone update
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "/home/myuser/.local/bin/tzupdate"

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Then ran `systemctl enable /home/myuser/.local/bin/tzupdate.service`, which has created the symlinks and made it to run automatically under Systemd.

However, this is apparently not sufficient in Ubuntu, and the script was run before there was Internet access. So, I have had to do the following modification:

```
[Unit]
Description=Time zone update
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "sleep 20; /home/myuser/.local/bin/tzupdate"

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Not as clean, but it works. I hope this is useful to other frequent flyers.

@kevin1024 what do you think about this pull request?

> Subsequent, as in any changes made to the settings after instantiating a VCR ?

Well, yes, after the instantiation of a VCR, but more importantly after the decoration of a function.

> Asking because I will include tests for this logic too.

Of course, I'll give you an example below that might help you write a test.

> Also, does this only work when use_cassette() is used as a decorator, and not in a with statement? Are there any caveats/differences to using the decorator vs. the with statement?

There aren't really any differences as far as implementation is concerned, but because a with statement is a piece of code that executes immediately, this kwarg can't really have an effect.

Let me provide an example that might help clarify:

``` python
vcr = VCR()

@vcr.use_cassette('some_file.yaml')
def some_test():
   ... do some stuff

@vcr.use_cassette('some_file.yaml', with_current_defaults=True)
def other_test():
   ... do some stuff

vcr.cassette_library_dir = 'some_directory_to_keep_cassettes_in'

some_test()
other_test()
```

In this example, the cassette will be saved in the  'some_directory_to_keep_cassettes_in' in the case of `some_test`, but not in the case of other_test. `with_current_defaults` is not relevant to context manager use because the code is always executed immediately, so there is no oppotunity to change vcr settings before the code is executed.


This may seem like a strange/unimportant feature, but its actually really important because a lot of test runners import tests very eagerly aggresively, and that is the point at which the decorator runs. If you wanted to have for example, a flag that sets the cassette library youd have to worry about whether the tests get imported before the code that sets the library directory.

Why on earth would you want to create a local copy of the whole FRED database? It's not going anywhere and it's reasonably quick, just call it as you need it.

I mean, you could do something like:

``
fred = fredapi.Fred(api_key=***)
for i in series_ids:
   fred.get_series(i).to_pickle("filename_for_series.pkl")
``

But the likelihood that the API would kick you out before it finished and block your API key is really, really high, for good reason-- you're basically doing a DoS attack on them.

Sure. Note: It's only when reconstituting the ast from json that the issue arises: converting from ast to json is not a problem.

Well, it does start with the ast to json conversion code in some sense. This code walks the tree and converts each node from a python object into a dict. Traversal uses the children() method. The children() method for each class has code that skips over empty child fields. e.g.:

`class Decl(Node):
    def __init__(self, name, quals, storage, funcspec, type, init, bitsize, coord=None):
        ...
        self.bitsize = bitsize

    def children(self):
        ...
        if self.bitsize is not None: nodelist.append(("bitsize", self.bitsize))`

Parsing funky.c for instance, there's a Decl which has no bitsize children. The resulting json has no mention of bitsize. This seems reasonable imo.

However, when reconstituting the ast from json, the code has to call the node contructors with all fields. Since the json doesn't have a "bitsize" key in this case, the contructor fails (IIRC it crashes with something like "8 parameters needed, only 7 given").

The "hack" is to use the slots field to fill in any missing key, value pairs in the kwargs with None values. It's not a terrible hack really, just a use of the slots field that's not its normal purpose.

A couple approaches occurred to me. The first, which I implemented, was to use the slots field to discover all fields the constructor would need. A second, which I mentioned, would involve default value of None in the constructors. Another approach which now occurs to me would be to change my code so that it introspects the class during the conversion to json, emitting null values for empty child fields rather than omitting them in the json.

Have you tried the Producer option `async_stop_timeout` ? This timeout parameter is passed into the async thread when the producer is created. Assignment changes are typically triggered by heartbeat responses telling the consumer that a rebalance is in progress. All of this happens internally within consumer.poll() -- there is no background thread. That means that you need to run the consumers independently for them to coordinate. Have you tried using multiple python repls / multiple processes?

I'd recommend multiprocessing for running parallel consumers. Python
threading is not useful given GIL.

**Version 3**, refactoring including reducing workload slightly

* Rather than the main loop checking which modules need updating modules 'notify' when they have updated.

* i3status modules self contained in a wrapper class I3statusModule.

* Module and I3statusModule expose same methods to the main loop. 

* Better separation of i3status and py3status modules.

* Lock no longer needed.

* i3status modules only trigger updates if content has changed.

* Although relocated the time correction logic for i3status modules is untouched.

Two thoughts:

- in determining *which list to use*, maybe make it explicit by using a ReST [directive](http://docutils.sourceforge.net/docs/ref/rst/directives.html). Potentially, this would allow you to have multiple changelogs within the Spinx generation (c.f. #59), and even within the same document. This would be a breaking change, and so should be part of the 2.0.0 release. In this case, you changelog would look like:

        Changes
        =======

        - list
        - items

        more text

        changelog::

            - :release:`0.1.0 <2017-02-07>`
            - :bug:`1` nothing yet

- in dealing with *errors related to release node being undefined* outside of a changelog list, would is be possible (simple?) to have the node function as a "pass-through" node, where the text would get passed through unchanged to the final document, which a warning is printed to the console? This is, in effect, how the nodes are treated is `releases` isn't installed.

This sounds like an edge case (until now, I can't say I have seen changelogs consisting 100% of non-numbered issues, they're usually mixed in with actual numbered issues) but one that I think should probably be legitimized. In 0ca39fd1b1558468a402f1cf8fa126ce4ed7e76a I removed the behavior that `pretty_midi` collapses tracks into instruments by program number.  So, it is now possible to give each `Instrument` a track name (if one appears) according to the track the instrument appears on.  Of course, this is not guaranteed to be a unique name because different instruments can appear on a given track (via channels and program changes).  This would be particularly problematic in type-0 MIDI files, where all events occur on a single track (but on different channels) - in this case, all `Instrument`s would be given the same name (i.e., the name of the sole track, assuming a track name event appears on it).  I think a sane solution would be to only populate the `Instrument` names if there is more than 1 track.  I'll reopen this with the intention of doing this eventually.

Okay, so looking at your simulate classmethod, there were a few errors introduced (but you made it a lot simpler and thus easy to spot!)

Firstly, I've changed the way simulate works to make it more 'physical'. Rather than adding the background at the end (as Gaussian noise), I've included the background sky in the initial image such that it is included via the Poisson noise term. The motivation here is that for a non-constant background sky this will now behave physical (albeit lets just assume for now the background sky is constant).

The code can thus simulate 3 effects, which are in order:

- Add background sky.
- Simulate optics via PSF.
- Add Poisson noise (including background sky)
- (subtract background sky, as AutoLens uses background subtracted images. This uses the input background sky map).

Previously, the method treated each effect independently. For example, the Poisson noise was added to the input array, NOT the input array after PSF blurring. I've rectified this, and added tests which correctly test the behaviour for multiple features being on / off.

As you'll see, the code is a bit messy now - mainly because I dont know how to use properties in a classmethod. However, it is still quite each to pick apart what is happening!

There will be more effects to add in future, but I think these 3 will suffice for making sure our simulate module works.

Here's how I solved it:
```python
lambda sequence: (lambda s: [wrap(s[i:])
                             for i in range(len(s) + 1)]
                 )(list(sequence)),
```

Length could me made more efficient by using `reduce(lambda acc, e: acc + 1, sequence, 0)`, but since there is a need for `[i:]` in the same function, it might just be better to do the conversion to list.

APScheduler does not support redundant schedulers or sharing of job stores. This will lead to problems. Instead, both processes should communicate with a single scheduler process using RPC or HTTP or a similar mechanism. APScheduler 4.0 *may* get support for redundant schedulers, but that's not certain.

> Hi, thank you for reporting this, but this problem is actually much more
> complex than it might seem at a glance.
>
> If you would like to have a point that represents a region or a subregion:
> where exactly do you want that point to be? The centroid of the
> region/subregion? What if that point is someplace far away from any city or
> sign of civilization? The capital of the region/subregion? Some places have
> multiple capitals - which one do I pick?
>
> If you're trying to find the distance between two regions/subregions:
> which points do you want to use? The distance between the centroids? That
> wouldn't ever really be accurate. The distance between the closest points
> on each region/subregion? That also wouldn't ever be accurate. The distance
> between the farthest points on each region/subregion? Also wouldn't ever be
> accurate.
>
> Really, representing an entire area as a point is the fundamental problem.
> I probably won't ever implement that for something as large as a
> region/subregion. There's simply no reasonable and generic way to represent
> an area as a point.
>
> So lets talk about areas (or "boundaries" in GIS terminology). I would
> absolutely *love* to add boundaries to all of the models in the app!
> However, there isn't really a single good source for that data.
> Specifically I would like a single source that is:
>
>    - international - only the US has very good maps (put out by the USGS
>    - thank you!), the rest of the world has no good maps
>    - accurate - I would like accurate sources
>    - legally friendly - the terms and conditions for using some maps
>    preclude them from use in this project - some sources may require payment,
>    even to download or use a sample, or they may require the use of closed
>    source or expensive software to use
>    - consistent - one single file format
>

In order to release a Python 3-compatible wheel on PyPI, you have to create a `setup.cfg` file with the following contents:

```
[bdist_wheel]
universal = 1
```

Then `python setup.py bdist_wheel upload` does the right thing.

You could also upload a source distribution with `python setup.py sdist upload`. That will allow installation on Python 3.

> Last thought, and here I'm possibly missing something cause I'm not able to get my head around it. Aren't refresh_tokens redundant?! I mean, chances are that in your application you end up storing both, the access_token and the refresh_token, in the same location (local storage, cookies, whatever). So if an attacker can get hold of one it will be able to get hold of the other as well, right?

That's very true. It's mostly additional security for protecting your tokens over the wire. If your access token goes out with every request, but the refresh token only goes out every once in awhile, there is less surface area for the refresh token to be stolen (given, this isn't very likely to be the attack vector in the first place, but still).

The other additional benefit for this is mostly for ease of using the backend. By separating access and refresh tokens it gives us the possibility to easily only check refresh tokens against the blacklist and limit the amount of checks you make against it (if one were to go that route). I'm sure this could be done with just access tokens as well, but it is just easier to manage and think about this way, at least in my opinion.

Anytime! :D I love being able to chat about stuff like this, it's a great way to learn about new ideas in the ever evolving field of security (and I am positive there is more security stuff like this out there for me to learn!)

Cheers :)

However, now the issue with string interpolation in `ConfigParser` which I have described in #152 remains. For example, if I have the following `.pylintrc` file and use it with _pylint-odoo_:
```ini
[ODOOLINT]
manifest_version_format=(%(valid_odoo_versions)s)\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$
valid_odoo_versions=10.0
```
Then due to the string interpolation in `ConfigParser` by the time [`formatversion()`](https://github.com/OCA/pylint-odoo/blob/a14ddca6f0d57e603c55f60b0128b129ad5fb81e/pylint_odoo/checkers/no_modules.py#L657) is called `self.config.manifest_version_format` will already be formatted to `(10.0)\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$` and the `re.escape`'d version numbers will simply be ignored by the `%` function (non-existent placeholders do not raise an exception). So version numbers like `10+0.1.0.0` will still be able to slip by with such a config file :/

One example use case for us is running Security Monkey (https://github.com/Netflix/security_monkey). It expects to use an instance role for credentials but our organisation doesn't currently allow using roles due to the maintenance overheard of creating/maintaining/securing for hundreds of users/applications. So we were using credentials file instead. 

The problem is made worse due to the way that the default session is cached... Security Monkey uses APScheduler to run 'watchers' every 15 minutes. So new threads are spinning up and calling boto3.resource('ec2') but they are getting the already cached session with the expired credentials. In this case if the credentials were reread (or the session wasn't cached) when the boto3 resource or client was instantiated everything would work.

Most algorithms in `scikit-optimize` are a bit tricky to parallelise (beyond say four or so instances). Basic reasoning: we look at past results to pick "the best" next point. So if we have to pick the 16 next points now to evaluate in parallel we will make a worse decision than if we do four batches of four where we can use the feedback of earlier batches when picking the next batch.

Yes ideally we wanted to have a single schema but also to reuse the ones we already have (complete them if necessary) since they are part of Kodi. `Include` revealed to be a problem as everything is in the same namespace and referring to elements is only possible with the `ref` attribute (which performs the lookup by name). So this was basically a decision to reduce the maintainability of the schemas and to have something working by the time the GSOC project ends.

Hopefully, at some point in the future and with xsd 1.1 we can have a better approach. One that can rely on the `test` attribute to check for different extension schemas depending on the extension `point` and requiring minimal changes to each of the extension schemas we already have. The problem is that there are no python tools at the moment that are able to validate xsd 1.1 (the `xmlschema` module we are using have in on the roadmap though).

What we are doing to validate the schemas is not ideally but it was a pondered decision. If you have a better idea/suggestion let us know @tamland.

> the pixels are indeed random colors

Not so much "random", rather it's called subpixel rendering. If set up properly, it typically improves readability. If set up improperly (e.g. GTK's belief about the order of subpixels is incorrect, perhaps you've rotated your monitor) then it might easily look terrible. Or just look up how to disable subpixel rendering in GTK if that's your preference.

An inherent problem of talking about subpixel rendering issues is that no matter how good or bad it looks for you, a screenshot doesn't convey this: on my computer it looks exactly as it would look with Guake on _my_ computer, not _yours_.

Yep, damn features. I think this particular approach is a bust. 

The good news is I can use the solver to enforce a simple rule: *no installations*, *no downgrades*. So:

- Specified package: add to the remove list
- Non-specified, non-installed package: add to the remove list (The solver doesn't care that they are not already installed---but this will prevent them from _being_ installed.)
- Non-specified, installed package: enforce a `>=`*ver* constraint, where *ver* is the current version. Also set its "target" so that the current package and version is the "best" choice. So in theory, it's still possible for the package to be *upgraded*, but not downgraded; and it will only be upgraded if it *has* to be.

That last behavior might actually be rather nice. Consider a package that has been modified so that it no longer needs a dependency. Removing the dependency would force the to be upgraded.

I'll close this PR when I have one that supercedes it.

In all of 2.7, 3.4, 3.5, PYTHONIOENCODING is not needed.  Python uses codepage encoding automatically.

When stdout is console, there are no reason to use it on Windows.
On *nix, it is useful when we use C/POSIX locale but we want UTF-8.  (This situation is cared automatically from Python 3.7).

When stdout is redirected, Python 2 used ascii for sys.stdout.encoding.
So `print u"こんにちは"` can be raise UnicodeEncodeError when stdout is redirected.
This is fixed by Python 3.2.

```
$ python2 -c 'import sys; print(sys.stdout.encoding)'
UTF-8

$ python2 -c 'import sys; print(sys.stdout.encoding)' | cat
None

$ python3 -c 'import sys; print(sys.stdout.encoding)' | cat
UTF-8
```

@EvanCarroll ,

Thank you for your thorough and thoughtful response.

> I would not ship out a binary zip of sqltoolsservice. That not a good idea. First on the matter of security, it's super sketch. We essentially have to trust Microsoft, and unless everything in that zip is a repeatable build, we really have to trust you. Moreover, how will security patches in sqltoolsservice tool work? They're going to notify any project using this distribution mechanism to rebuild against them? Doesn't this entirely invalidate the idea of shared libraries?

**Response:** mssql-cli does not ship zipped binaries of sqltoolsservice, but rather the official binaries of the latest github release of sqltoolsservice per supported distro/OS. If you disagree with the distribution format of sqltoolsservice, I would recommend filing a issue there as I don't know the entire details of their distribution route. 

One thing that I really liked from your suggestion was the idea of how to track which sqltoolsservice was released with mssql-cli, we can update our release notes and provide some mechanism for versioning to verify the release match up.

> Provide build scripts for your Microsoft maintainers inside of sqltoolsservice that push up the essential parts to PyPy that mssql-cli requires.

**Response:** Originally when we release mssql-scripter we had a individual sqltoolsservice package hosted on PYPI. This was redesigned after because:
1. **Maintenance and dependency**
    With more than 1 python tool relying on sqltoolsservice, we decided it would be best for each tool to be self contained and ship it's own necessary version of sqltoolsservice. This prevents any cross version dependency where if a user has both mssql-cli and mssq-scripter installed, and sqltoolsservice introduces a breaking change, we wouldn't want to break both tools. Rather we want each tool to operate independently with only the latest necessary sqltoolsservice.

   Your suggestion of shipping only the necessary parts of sqltoolsservice on PYPI may even lead to more confusion because if another tool needs a different part of the library, the sqltoolsservice python package would have to be renamed because PYPI only supports unique package names. Multiplying this by 7 - 12 packages of sqltoolsservice per OS/Distro leads to a maintainers nightmare. 

2. **Wheels Wheels Wheels**
    If we went the route of hosting sqltoolsservice on PYPI (after we get through the troubles mentioned above), we would have 7 - 12 sqltoolsservice python packages each containing their own dll's and OS specific libraries. In order to ship mssql-cli or mssql-scripter both tools would require platform detection during install time to pull down the right version and name of sqltoolsservice for host OS. Being self contained allows us to upload wheels rather then our source code to PYPI and decreases install time.

the new abs error with script magic by @adriesse 
```python
from pvlib.solarposition import (
    solar_azimuth_analytical, get_solarposition, solar_zenith_analytical,
    hour_angle, declination_spencer71, equation_of_time_spencer71
)
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
plt.ion()

dt = pd.DatetimeIndex(start='2017-01-01T00:00:00-0800',
                      end='2017-12-31T23:59:59-0800', freq='H')

LAT = np.arange(0, 76, 1.0, dtype=float)
LON = -120
abserr = []

# this is the magic by Anton Driesse, to detect where to shift the circle
def angle_difference(a1, a2):
    da = a2 - a1
    da = np.where(da > 180, da-360, da)
    da = np.where(da<=-180, da+360, da)
    return da


for lat in LAT:
    sp = get_solarposition(time=dt, latitude=lat, longitude=LON)

    decl = declination_spencer71(dt.dayofyear)  # radians
    eot = equation_of_time_spencer71(dt.dayofyear)  # minutes
    hr = hour_angle(times=dt, longitude=LON, equation_of_time=eot)  # degrees

    ze = solar_zenith_analytical(np.radians(lat), np.radians(hr), decl)
    az = solar_azimuth_analytical(np.radians(lat), np.radians(hr), decl, ze)

    sp['analytical_zenith'] = np.degrees(ze)
    sp['analytical_azimuth'] = np.degrees(az)
    sp['daytime'] = sp['zenith'] < 90.0

    sp['az_reldiff'] = angle_difference(sp['azimuth'], sp['analytical_azimuth']) / sp['azimuth']
    sp['ze_reldiff'] = 1 - sp['zenith'] / sp['analytical_zenith']
    abserr.append(np.abs(sp[['az_reldiff', 'ze_reldiff']][sp.daytime]).mean())

plt.figure()
plt.plot(LAT, abserr, '-o')
plt.legend(['analytical azimuth abs. err.', 'analytical zenith abs. err.'])

np.abs(sp[['az_reldiff', 'ze_reldiff']][sp.daytime]).plot()
```

Yeah, testing wxPython is kind of a pain.  There might be some useful information in this post, but it might not be anything you don't already know: http://wiki.wxpython.org/Unit%20Testing%20with%20wxPython

The way I typically run the tests is with: "python -m unittest discover".  This means that the unittest module will find anything that calls unittest.main():

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        unittest.main()

We can instruct unittest to skip certain tests: https://docs.python.org/2/library/unittest.html#skipping-tests-and-expected-failures .  For example, the code below can be applied at the class or the test-case level, and will skip everything in that class/test case.

    @unittest.skip("Reason for skipping")
    class()....

There is also "@unittest.skipIf", so there might be some kind of conditional logic that we could apply.

@marcoabreu I tried to use below "try .. catch ..." statement to test native CPU, MKLDNN, GPU
```
for dtype in ['int8','uint8']:
    try:
        run_test(..., dtype)
    catch:
```
But for those OPs not supported by native CPU, the test would "segmentation fault" directly and the exception handler could not catch it so the test will abort directly and could not execute any further case, I think the MXNet framework didn't handle the unsupported OP very well (when OP didn't register FCompute/FComputeEx like for native CPU it didn't register FCompute/FComputeEx for quantized conv/pooling/fc), another case is if we test unsupported dtype for one backend (like test int8 for MKLDNN or uint8 for GPU), the C++ code will do parameter check failure and throw runtime error message, of course we can catch this exception and ignore it but it is not the specific OperatorMissingError exception.

I think it's not trivial to change MXNet C++ code to throw the specific exception for various unsupported cases (need to add proper exception thrower for all these cases), how about use below 2 approaches which makes life a little easier:

```
run_test(..., dtype)    // add checking at the top of run_test() so we can see what kind of test is expected to be skipped and the rest should be tested and passed
    if context == cpu_mkldnn:
        if dtype == 'int8'
            logging.info(skipping due to unsupported dtype {}, dtype)
            return
    elif context == cpu_native:
        ...

    <normal-test-path for dtype>

for dtype in ['int8','uint8']:
    run_test(..., dtype)
```
This approach tested all cases and skipped the unsupported case explicitly so users can see, for not skipped case they are supposed to test and pass through.
```
run_test(..., dtype)  // test case implementation
    <normal-test-code-path for dtype>

if context == cpu_native:    // list test cases for each context implicitly
    dtype_list = []    // empty means doesn't support this quantized OP
elif if context == cpu_mkldnn:
    dtype_list = [uint8] 
elif if context == gpu:
     dtype_list = [int8] 
for dtype in dtype_list:
        run_test(..., dtype)
```
This approach explicitly listed the dtype_list each context support and only test the supported scenario, we don't have backend info in the test case implementation( `run_test()` ) so the test case implementation will be clean.

I agree that move constructors should be preferred, but there's a bit of a tradeoff there. Due to Python's dynamic typing, pybind11 relies on runtime type information to properly cast stuff back and forth. C++'s static type information (lvalue vs rvalue, const vs mutable) is lost in the process. This information needs to be encoded into runtime data as well. This is already done for the actual objects, but not for the object holders. 

Most of the time this doesn't matter since most holders behave somewhat like `std::unique_ptr` or `std::shared_ptr`. The latter could benefit from using its move constructor, but copying is also pretty cheap compared to all the other runtime machinery that binds C++ and Python. That's true for most custom holders so I'm not sure optimizing for the corner case would be worth the code complication here.

Potentially one way to resolve this discussion is to simply not use ExtraFlags for case 1. IMHO the only "real" application of ``ExtraFlags`` has been the situation where we want to bind a function taking a ``py::array``, but the C++ code makes a lot of assumptions about how data is laid out, what type it has, etc. So then we can bind an ``array_t<float, array::f_style>`` to eliminate an annoying amount of boilerplate code. This approach could even be extended with some additional flags that will ensure that only 1D or 2D arrays can be supplied, etc.

Hi Riandy,

Thank for looking at the pull request.

> 3. Since in current version user could only delete messages by using `range`, essentially server will delete messages with seq id 1 until 300, one by one.

The user sends a delete request for a range {low: 1, hi: 301}. The rethinkdb adapter will receive the request to delete messages 1 <= seq < 301 and will delete the whole range at once: https://github.com/tinode/chat/blob/better-delete/server/db/rethinkdb/adapter.go#L979

The adapter will receive the ranges to delete just like they were sent by the client. It's up to the adapter to execute the delete strategy. For instance, all SQL databases can do an atomic `DELETE FROM messages WHERE seq BETWEEN 1 AND 301`. Mongo DB can do it with `db.collection.deleteMany()`

> 4. But if user could also delete messages by using `clear`, it just need to set the clear value on User A's subscription to topic X.

Yes, I removed this option. That was the old soft-delete method. It was only good for "hide all messages with IDs below `clear`". It could not be used to delete individual messages by IDs, which is one of the most common use cases.

> on DynamoDB which update operation is restricted to only 1 update per request, it is really troublesome.

Yes, I can see how this could be a problem. 

What if instead we tell the user which items were actually deleted:
User asks to delete `{low: 1, hi: 301}` (or just `{hi: 301}`), DynamoDb adapter deletes 1 to 150 then fails. The adapter returns a "partial success", the server responds to the client with `{ctrl code=207 params={what="del", delseq={low: 1, hi: 151}}}`? This way the client understands that the operation was only partially successful.

Alternatively, since this is an unusual limitation specific to DynamoDB, you can solve it in the DynamoDB adapter by creating a separate table just for the `clear` or keeping a custom field like `dynamo_db_clear` on `subscriptions` in the existing schema. When reading the messages in `MessageGetAll ` you can just lookup the `clear` value from that place. It will be a cheap lookup by the primary key. The adapter owns the storage schema. You can modify it for you case in any way you see fit. It's an optimization specific to the adapter. Adapter should own it. 

At a high level there are only three ways of dealing with non-atomic backend operations:

* Have client issue only such requests which can be performed atomically by the server.
* Have server report partial success. If the operation executed partially, let the client know which parts were executed and which failed. Let the client clean up the mess. For instance, the client can just reissue the request.
* Same as above but try to clean up the mess on the server.

Just a quick update on performance notes... the other I day I wrote

> The producer seems to be bottlenecked on the produce() method

It turns out that that is not true - I jumped to conclusions too quickly because the work that happens in callbacks on background threads isn't very visible when doing (too simplistic) profiling and benchmarking. What's really expensive, it turns out, is calling `Future.set_result()` (which happens in a callback on a background thread) and also `concurrent.futures.wait()`. I called `wait()` as part of the benchmark run, which turns out to more than double the run time (in retrospect that's maybe not surprising, because `Future` builds on `threading.Condition` which builds on `threading.RLock`, so waiting and setting results on a 100k long list of futures just means a lot of locking/unlocking).

If we immediately discard futures (that is, we just drop the return values from `Producer.produce()`) then `RdKafkaProducer` runs at about 2x the rate in messages/sec (for now only informally tested, with small message sizes) of `pykafka.Producer` (when we also discard futures in that benchmark).
And still, nearly 25% of that time is spent instantiating/discarding unused futures...

@embray @astrofrog - I wanted to report an oddity related to this issue that I just encountered.  Halotools (which uses the package template) was getting strange errors on travis while trying to run their test suite.  It turns out this is because they were using numpy v1.8 for the main part of the test suite, and the conda packages for astropy are numpy 1.9 *only* after v4.1.  So they were getting astropy v4.1, which is incompatible with the current astropy helpers (due to the addition of the ``repeat`` keyword for the test runner).  Having all of the test stuff in the helpers would get around such versioning issues.

We're continuing to work on getting an official Ubuntu 14.04 PPA (see #1706), however, until that's ready, I can continue to try and develop a workaround for you guys. The reason it's a bit tricky is I've been completely unable to reproduce the problem, however, to build off my most recent suggestion above, does this work:

In the meantime, you can follow the instructions below. Replace `venv_dir` with the absolute path to where you want the Python virtual environment used to run Certbot to be saved.

1. Download `certbot-auto` and run `./certbot-auto --os-packages-only`
2. Run `virtualenv --no-site-packages --python $(command -v python2.7 || command -v python27 || command -v python2 || command -v python) venv_dir`
3. Run `venv_dir/bin/pip install -U setuptools`
4. Run `venv_dir/bin/pip install urllib3 certbot-apache`

Now you can run Certbot by running `venv_dir/bin/certbot`. You can make this easier by putting a symlink to this file somewhere in your path.

```
Opaque enums are phased out from the cdefs: they now give a warning, instead of (possibly wrongly) being assumed equal to unsigned int. Please report if you get a reasonable use case for them.
```

I  believe that change is what is resulting in the warnings, in the header file ```pygit2/decl.h```.

I found a fix. When we serialize a floating point number, we need to make sure it outputs it at the max precision. By default, it will round the float to a few decimal places.

~~~c++
outStream << myFloat < " "; // BAD

outStream.precision(std::numeric_limits<Real>::max_digits10);
outStream << myFloat < " "; // GOOD
~~~

If we change `SpatialPooler::save` to do this, the test passes.

This happens because the `AuthenticationMiddleware`, which sets the `user` on the `request` expects to use the session store: https://github.com/django/django/blob/0f7f5bc9e7a94ab91c2b3db29ef7cf000eff593f/django/contrib/auth/__init__.py#L159.

DRF-JWT (this module) uses PyJWT for decode and encode.  PyJWT only looks for `iat` in the payload ([here](https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/b35d522135044ba10ac41e7db5b95348cb4c4707/jwt/api_jwt.py#L44)) and since it doesn't find it, `iat` will is never encoded into the key to begin with.  Hence the client will never get the `iat` anyways.  Therefore, `orig_iat` is a no-op here.

So, to be clear: you have measured that, in your usage, any potential gains of retaining learned clauses is sufficiently important. Otherwise, the typical observation that I experience is that internalizing formulas and relearning lemmas is a relative small overhead compared to search. The two main portions where state can be captured are the sat_solver and smt_solver cores. They wrap into different solver objects, where the sat_solver is now getting available for incremental use as well. The main incremental object so far has been the smt_solver context. Cloning it seems rather error prone to program because you would want to maintain the associating between expressions and internal representations (e-nodes and clauses), and ultimately, I am not sure there is a real use case for it.

Not sure how to answer directly. 
Exporting to DIMACS seems like a diversion from the purpose you are stating.
The general statement is that you can extract cores for the set of assertions you track. 
You decide which assertions are tracked and Z3 will report cores over tracked assertions. 
Alternatively you can use indicator variables with clauses and check satisfiability with the indicator variables as assumptions. The SMT-LIB2.6 format describes the format for using such indicator variables.
A SAT solver doesn't really give you anything more: the use of indicator variables is based on an approach developed by MiniSAT and adapted widely. With clausal proofs you may dispense with these variables and scale better, but in terms of basic capabilities they are the same with Z3.

Note that |B89| is a macro for a conjunction using three literals. These are not really functions. They are propositional identifiers. "define-fun" acts as a macro definition. "declare-fun" can be used for 0-ary functions, in other words as placeholder for "declare-const", which are treated as uninterpreted identifiers (I tend to call them uninterpreted constants).

Suppose you produce a data for websocket very often.
Suppose the connection to websocket peer is slow.
If `send_str()` is not a coroutine the method call doesn't wait for actual data sending but stores the data into an internal buffer.
Shortly it leads to out-of-memory error.

Since `send_str()` is a coroutine -- call it from other coroutine as `await ws.send_str(data)`.

You need two `notify_all()` versions: one for async code and another for usage from other threads.
`run_until_complete()` cannot be called from a coroutine.
`get_event_loop()` returns a current thread's loop. not the global system one.

You cannot use bare `run_coroutine_threadsafe()` exactly for the same reason as `send_str()` is not a non-blocking function. To avoid potential memory error you need to wait for returned future.

Note: the `sentential -- stopping` line is on STDERR.

Some things about your example, though:

* Why bother with the `islice()`? When you open a file handle, reading from the file is already iterative. In fact, when you do `handle.read()`, you are loading the full contents of the file into memory, which negates any (redundant) benefits of creating a Python-generator over it. You might as well just do `handle.readlines()[:100]`
* Does `isplit()` split lines or tokens? ACE expects full lines (sentences) as input, generally.
* Why do you use the `-X` option, which ACE itself [does not recommend](http://moin.delph-in.net/AceOptions)? I think using `-X` negates the effect of `-1`. The former returns the *first* result that ACE finds, while the latter returns the *top* result based on the parse ranking model. I don't know which is returned if you use both (probably the first, not (necessarily) the top).

I think a simpler calling of `parse_from_iterable()` would be less prone to errors:

```python
>>> from delphin.interfaces import ace
>>> with open('ex.txt') as fh:
...     for response in ace.parse_from_iterable(
...             '../../grammars/erg-1214-x86-64-0.9.27.dat',
...             fh,
...             executable='/opt/ace-0.9.27/ace',
...             cmdargs=['-1','--timeout', '7']):
...         # do something with response
```

Also, while looking into this problem, I uncovered a bug when a blank line is sent to ACE. See #155.

Regarding `spv_path` in `[bitcoind]`, this is set by `virtualchain`, not by Blockstack.  Blockstack's API daemon, its indexer, and Virtualchain all share this file.  This is why it's outside `~/.blockstack`.  In the future, this particular field should be set in some global config file like `/etc/virtualchain.conf` or the like.

I'll remove `blockchain_headers` from `[blockstack-client]`.  That file stores the exact same data as `~/.virtualchain-spv-headers.dat`.  The fact that they're separate is an artifact of legacy code (i.e. before Virtualchain had an SPV client).

The rest of the paths that start with `/Users/larry` should reflect your choice of config file path with `--config`.  I'll fix those.

OK, if we're loading objects from disk with extra information we need a mechanism for preserving this data in a read-only manner.
This should be something we agree on so I'll assume this to be true.

As for the safety, major != safe, minor == safe, I don't know why this is so bad, tbh, we can even drop the minor here if we build in a backwards compatible way of preserving data.
'
'
In fact I'd like to propose we fix preserving information not known to the current schema and keep the major version information to resolve compatibility issues and drop the minor version support as this serves no useful purpose if the job is compatible. As controversial as this may be.

'
'
I'll explain my concerns wrt compatibility below:


My problem with dropping all version checking is that that the only check that is performed when loading an attribute into memory is to test the type against the allowed types in the schema def.
If the class wants/needs to change the contents of the object which is stored then there is no sensible way to add support for this unless we have every class do the checking itself which largely does away with the whole point of schema system.

I know that statement is probably a bit strong but if the class can't write 'supercali...' to where it used to write 'foo:bar' then there is no easy way to convey that the expectations of the class have changed.

Yes the class can add a new attribute then we end up with 'stale' schema attributes which aren't being used or are only required for loading old jobs through ganga.
This leaves the schema cluttered and confusing which I'm not in favour of.

Also when an older object is loaded and manipulated it is always written with the version of the schema used at runtime so future Ganga versions would always upgrade an object potentially into a format that cannot be read by older versions.

eg:

Ganga v7r1 stores the attribute {myData, myGangaClassv1}
Ganga v7r2 can read myGangaClassv1 but now has myGangaClassv2. It can also translate the classes to the newer and better myGangaClassv2 which is largely a superset of myGangaClassv2 but is different enough to not be backwards compatible.

user now creates a job in v7r1, tries out v7r2 and doesn't like it and now attempts to go back to v7r1.

The job they created in 7r1 now has the attribute {myData, myGangaClassv2} which causes all jobs to now not be accessible.
The user cannot use v7r2 due to a new bug or other incompatibility and cannot use v7r1 due to the fact that their jobs have now been mangled by Ganga v7r2 which could read them.
The problem is that Ganga v7r2 has a very good reason to be using myGangaClassv2 because it addresses an issue.

I would like a major version number to allow us to ask the user if they want the object to be upgraded/loaded to prevent occurrences such as this.

According to [section 2.c of the feature file specification](http://www.adobe.com/devnet/opentype/afdko/topic_feature_file_syntax.html#2.c), the compiler would be wrong if it accepted lowercase `dflt` for a language tag.

>  DFLT  # can be used only with the script keyword and as the script value with the languagesystem keyword. 
>  dflt  # can be used only with the language keyword and as the language value with the languagesystem keyword.

Indeed, the [OpenType specification](https://www.microsoft.com/typography/otspec/CHAPTER2.htm) writes:

> If a Script table with the script tag 'DFLT' (default) is present in the ScriptList table, it must have a non-NULL DefaultLangSys and LangSysCount must be equal to 0. The 'DFLT' Script table should be used if there is not an explicit entry for the script being formatted.

Now, the binary font file NotoNastaliqUrdu-Regular.ttf contains a script tag `dflt`:

```xml
      <ScriptRecord index="1">
        <ScriptTag value="dflt"/>
        <Script>
          <DefaultLangSys>
            <ReqFeatureIndex value="65535"/>
            <!-- FeatureCount=6 -->
            <FeatureIndex index="0" value="0"/>
            <!-- ... -->
          </DefaultLangSys>
          <!-- LangSysCount=0 -->
        </Script>
      </ScriptRecord>
```

In other words, the input font is broken and should be fixed. Instead of `dflt`, it should use `DFLT` as the script tag. Filed https://github.com/googlei18n/noto-fonts/issues/805. Closing this bug because the pipeline is correct in rejecting this input.

"Factoring out the duplication..." not I sure I catch your meaning?   Making them default sources in some custom target type?

So, treating these two things separately:

- Targets with no sources at all
- Individual globs that match nothing, but at least one matches something

### Targets with no sources at all:

Examples of cases where a target with no sources can legitimately exist:

- I've temporarily removed its only source file, for debugging.
- I generate project structure (e.g., a Django project) and have yet to add source files in all the places.

But I get how in other cases, a target with no sources indicates a bug, and you'd want to flag that.

### Individual globs with no matches

I'm feeling that an individual glob in a list of globs not matching anything is less likely to indicate a bug. I mean, it might, but there are so many other ways you can screw up creating your globs, such as entirely forgetting about certain files, or globbing over too many files, that it seems unnecessary to me to finesse this one special case.

But my underlying point is that I think we should be moving away from forcing people to carefully craft targets, except in exceptional cases.   The only thing worse than boilerplate is boilerplate than you then have to fine-tune...  Default sources were a step in a good direction, this feels like a step back, in some ways.  

Copy-pasting is, in practice, how most targets are created. So the workflow in my example now has to be: "copy-paste some other web resources target, then think about whether it has .css or .js files, or both, or neither, and edit appropriately, and then if you ever add those in the future, revisit this globs appropriately". I think we should be moving towards "90% of the time we already know what to do, so just do that"...

### To get practical though

Let's at least decouple these two orthogonal concepts: "default sources" and "sources that are allowed to be empty". 

So that instead of checking for the former, we check for the latter, and the former is implemented with the "these sources are allowed to be empty" flag set to True, or whatever.  Then this functionality can be reused outside the realm of default sources. For example, a script that generates project structure (like in my Django example) can set that flag even on non-default sources (a user can than manually set it to False in the generated BUILD file later on if it seems right to do so).

Does that make sense?

Here's multiline commands in a nutshell. To the user, instead of <return> completing your entry of the statement, you have to have an unquoted semicolon (or two <return> characters in a row). If you are familiar with how SQL commands in the `mysql` command line program work, that's pretty much how they work in `cmd2`.

Internally, when the user presses <return> the parsing logic checks if the first word on the line is one of the multi-line commands. If it is, and if the terminator character is not found in the input, then `cmd2` changes the prompt, and lets the user keep typing. This repeats until the terminator character is found, after which we join all the lines together and then fully parse the whole thing.

I had vaguely heard about the ``wrapt`` module, but had never looked into it.  It sort of sounds like an improved version of ``functools.wraps`` which sounds like a great thing to me.  

If we are already moving in the direction of adding more 3rd-party dependencies on things like [attrs](https://github.com/python-attrs/attrs), then I don't see any reason to not replace our current usage of ``functools.wraps`` with the ``wrapt`` module.

At one point I believe we were having an internal debate regarding whether we wanted to try to minimize 3rd-party dependencies to move in the direction of possibly having a **cmd2-lite** variant which could eventually replace the current built-in ``cmd`` module.  However, I don't believe any of the current ``cmd2`` maintainers were willing to step up and assume the responsibility of becoming a Python Core Maintainer for the ``cmd`` module.  

The alternative possibility is to continue to strive to be the best possible tool for quickly and easily building feature-rich interactive command-line applications and in that case it is fine to embrace adding 3rd-party dependencies on popular Python modules which are in wide use.  Of course, we have other projects which have ``cmd2`` as a dependency such as OpenStack [cliff](https://github.com/openstack/cliff) so adding dependencies can potentially impact these projects and we shouldn't go too nuts.

```
Some processes that the driver needs to connect to have not registered with Redis, so retrying. Have you run 'ray start' on this node?
```

it looks like **machine 2** is successfully connecting to **Redis**. However, to run `ray.init(redis_addres=...)` on a machine, it has to also connect to a few other processes (object store, object manager, and local scheduler) *on the same machine* (so machine 2 in this case) via Unix domain sockets. Those processes are started when you run `ray start` on a machine, so right now Ray expects `ray start` to be run on any machine where you are calling `ray.init(redis_addres=...)`. Note that if you don't want any tasks to be scheduled on that machine, you can specify `ray start --num-cpus=0`.

In terms of ports that need to be opened, the object stores also communicate with each other over TCP. The port is chosen randomly but can be specified on each machine with `ray start --object-manager-port=1234`. Also, there are multiple Redis servers on the head node, so we need to be able to communicate with all of them. In this case `40452` and `53790`. The IP address of the "primary" shard can be specified with `ray start --redis-port=6379`, but right now there is no way to specify the ports of the other shards.

Can you elaborate a bit on the use case? In particular, would it be possible to just run `ray start` locally?

This could (almost) already be achieved by using

```python
ready_ids, _ = ray.wait(object_ids, timeout=...)
ray.get(ready_ids)
```

The differences are that

1. This involves a little more code.
2. This involves an extra IPC round trip to the object store.
3. Some of the objects corresponding to the object IDs in `ready_ids` could in principle be evicted from the object store between between the `ray.wait` and `ray.get` call, causing the `ray.get` call to block for a little while.

Well it turns out that this is a fun one. `'nested_admin'` must be before `'django.contrib.admin'` in `INSTALLED_APPS`. If it comes after then `all_valid()` does not get patched by monkey business. The test suite runs with `'nested_admin'` as the first entry.

Maybe a documentation update is required. By convention most Django projects list Django apps, third party apps then their own apps.

I'm also wondering if we should not represent both syscall and function ABIs in a single object. For example if we wanted to implement Cdecl and Fastcall ABI classes for x86, both of those share the same syscall ABI since its x86 Linux, so might need to be duplicated code. Although, this can be solved with a class hierarchy like `ABI` -> `LinuxX86(ABI)` (implement syscall stuff) -> `LinuxCdecl(LinuxX86)` (implement func call stuff)

> To debug the problem, I need to make a Django application calculating a factorial number. But I don't know about Django at all. Can you teach me how can I make it?

Sure.

You may create a django project  with `django-admin startproject factorial`. Then, in the inner 'factorial' directory (where urls.py, settings.py, etc. exist) create a 'views.py' file that looks like this:
```Python
from django.http import HttpResponse
import math

def factorial(request, n):
    n = int(n)
    return HttpResponse(math.factorial(n))
```

Last, make the 'urls.py' file look like this:
```Python
from django.conf.urls import url
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^(?P<n>[0-9]+)', views.factorial),
]
```

Hi @miaodahua. The default asserts in SeleniumBase are self.assert_text() and self.assert_element(). However, the self.check_assert_text() and and self.check_assert_element() are special methods that were build to handle the case of doing multiple assertions on the same page in-a-row without failing the test immediately until all assertions have been completed. At the end of your check_assert methods, you need to call self.process_checks() which will then run all the assertions at once and fail the test if any have passed. (See https://github.com/seleniumbase/SeleniumBase/blob/68c6ff9013a7ab422f63d6a8ddb269d6df863ec3/seleniumbase/fixtures/base_case.py#L1172)

This feature was built to save time during testing. If a web page being tested had multiple failures, then rather than failing the test right away on the first failure (and then having to make a fix before continuing through the rest of the test) now, instead, the test would report all the failures together, once self.process_checks() was called.

In many cases, you might want the test to fail immediately after the first failed assertion, in which case you should use self.assert_text() and self.assert_element() instead. But if you use the "check_assert_" methods, you must use self.process_checks() or else the test will not fail. Does this help?

I'm fairly sure that this is due to the issues from #79 and #78.   You can see that the error comes from the failed picking of the `int64` object, I think for reasons as noted in the above-referenced tickets.  Note that it's also been fixed if you change the `settings` to use `'recurse'` mode.
```
>>> import dill
>>> from sympy import Symbol, lambdify
>>> x = Symbol("x")
>>> H = lambdify(x, x, "numpy")
>>> dill.detect.errors(H)
PicklingError("Can't pickle <type 'numpy.int64'>: it's not the same object as numpy.int64",)
```

Add `r` before the string constant.

To clarify, what the problem is. Let's say you create a Lua script in Python using this code:

```py
script = """x = [[ window.foo='\n' ]]"""
```

This is what you get (it is just how Python works):

```
In [5]: print(script)
x = [[ window.foo='
' ]]
```

So, you need to prevent  "\n" from converting to a new line. You can do it by escaping, by not defining a script as a string constant, or by using raw strings:

```
In [6]: script = r"""x = [[ window.foo='\n' ]]"""
In [7]: print(script)
x = [[ window.foo='\n' ]]
```

Ok, this is better now. Now we may have exactly the same issue in Lua code: x is a string constant, and Lua handles `\n` the same way as Python, replacing it with a new line. But we don't have it, because Lua only replaces "\n" in strings defined using quotes; strings defined using [[ ]] don't interpret escape sequences.

Alternative to this fix if capture=False is used [here](https://github.com/singularityhub/singularity-cli/blob/db78211fe53249d89d39c611d68ab17b75d77ba8/spython/instance/cmd/start.py#L68) then this problem would go away

In theory fixing Travis on Python 3.2 is easy: add a build step that looks like
```yaml
- if [ $TOXENV = py32 ]; then pip install 'virtualenv<14' 'pip<8'; fi
```

(for projects that have a matrix based on Python versions instead of TOXENVs the check would be `if [ $TRAVIS_PYTHON_VERSION = 3.2 ]`)

The question is: should we do that (maybe in a for loop for all projects), or should we just remove Python 3.2 support.

Anyway, I want to get that sorted out in master before accepting pull requests, because an unhealthy CI is a sign of an unhealthy project.

I recommend using Django's `inspect.py` module to check if the callable accepts `request`:

https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.11/django/utils/inspect.py

This strategy has been used internally by Django to great success. Here is one example:

https://github.com/django/django/commit/be6ca89396c031619947921c81b8795d816e3285

I think if the callable does not accept `request` a deprecation warning should be emitted so library users know their code must change to remain compatible with future versions.

```python
class SquarePipeline(pipeline.Pipeline):

    def run(self, number):
        logging.info('Squaring: %s' % number)
        return number * number


class Sum(pipeline.Pipeline):

    def run(self, *args):
        value = sum(list(args))
        logging.info('Sum: %s', value)
        return value


class FanInPipeline(pipeline.Pipeline):

    def run(self, count):
        results = []
        for i in xrange(0, count):
            result = yield SquarePipeline(i)
            results.append(result)

        # Waits until all SquarePipeline results are complete
        yield Sum(*results)
```
In my own pipeline there is nothing more than this fan-in implementation.  
 
For this example (if the my problem happens to it) I logged throw the loop and found that the loop has called the SquarePipeline successfully for all the items 
But some of the SquarePipeline  pipelines do not start at all ( the  logging I put inside the run method of SquarePipeline has never been called for some missing pipelines).

I can overcome the problem by increasing the number of retries for the queue in queue.yaml but i think this is not a good thing to rely on without understanding the problem.

regarding finalize method I there is no finalize implementation for it. 
regarding local environment the pipelines are working fine also in small subsets of data, but in my app the data is more bigger (the loop in FanInPipeline is generating in some cases more than 100 of pipelines) and then the problem arises, which makes me not satisfied with increasing number of retries solution until I understand the source of the problem.

The `Iterator` is already the response, so people want to be able to say:

```python
for project in client.list_projects():
    do_something(project)
```

where `client.list_projects()` is an instance of an `Iterator` subclass.

Also, by using indirection via punting to `iterator.pages()` and `iterator.items()`, does the `Iterator` instance hold any state? Does it know where it is in the iteration process? What happens if someone does this:

```python
page_iter = iterator.pages()
items_iter = iterator.items()
page = next(page_iter)
items = list(page)
next_item = next(items_iter)
```

Rather than get in the "What if?", the current implementation says: "we only iterate through items, but if you want to know about (the current page / the current place in the current page / the current page number / etc.) then we will tell you".

> If the IP were recognized as a "local host", it would set the "local host" option, and then this library's DatastoreFactory takes care of adding the scheme (and /datastore in the path).

When a user sets the host to "127.0.0.1:8000", `gcloud-java` correctly determines that the user is trying to set the local host.  For that reason, I don't think splitting up the local host option in `gcloud-java` or passing IP addresses to the local host option would solve this particular problem.  

To clarify what I was saying before, it's not possible to supply raw IP addresses to the local host option in this library because `validateUrl` requires scheme (`new URI("some IP address without scheme")` will throw an exception) but this library's `DatastoreOptions` won't accept scheme for localhost.

@ajtritt Got it, thanks!  My two cents is that this method deserves a different name.  By looking at your gist and guessing at what it does, it might be better to call it something like: 

```Python
get_hdfio_serializer(nwbfile, foo_ext_path)
```

This might seem like a trivial thing (please don't take this wrong, only trying to be productive, not sniping!), but methods should be named according to what they do.  Also, this frees up the to_hdf5 method for an extremely lightweight implementation that could provide the UX that I think @neuromusic could get behind:

```Python
def to_hdf5(self, nwbfile, foo_ext_path):
    io = pynwb.get_hdfio_serializer(nwbfile, foo_ext_path)
    io.write(nwbfile)
    io.close()
```

This type of minimal extension to the class is easy enough to maintain, and in my opinion would provide a UX that is worth its maintenance costs.

As a side note, in either case, I would name this to_hdf instead (in analogy to pandas), so that it is easy to remember.
